data.name: Diksha's Resume
data.description: Rebuild Diksha's nursing resume in a modern two-column template (photo header, sidebar credentials, experience column) that she can edit herself in Google Docs.
data.nextStep: Neil re-uploads the compacted one-page docx (~/Downloads) to Google Docs and deletes the stale Docs copy. Then Diksha edits: swap in her photo, fill the [bracketed] placeholders (LinkedIn, RN license #, BLS date), trim bullets. Any layout tweaks: edit build_resume.py in the project folder and rebuild (workflow in its CLAUDE.md).
data.lastDid: Built the full template from her 2025 resume and iterated to v2: compact header with photo + name + one-line contact info, contact section removed from the sidebar, tighter spacing so everything fits one page (her Google Docs export of v1 had spilled to page 2). Docx is Google-Docs-import-safe (Arial, stripped to ~13KB) since Neil can't edit Word docs. Learned: don't upload docx via the Drive MCP tool (base64 gets corrupted) — Neil uploads manually.
data.notes:
• Template: BeamJobs 'Audrey Bailey RN' style Neil screenshotted — circular photo, two columns, gray palette
• All content copied verbatim from her old resume; she rewrites the words, we own the layout
• Everything is generated by build_resume.py (python-docx) — never hand-edit the docx
• Must stay editable in Google Docs (Neil has no Word)

log.text: Converted her 2025 resume into the new two-column template (v1), fixed Google Docs compatibility (Arial, no letter-spacing, shrunk file 40KB→13KB), then v2 compacted the header (contact line under the name, sidebar CONTACT section removed, tighter spacing) so it fits one page. Project folder created with scripts + CLAUDE.md handoff.

data.name: Dock Issue — iOS PWA raised-dock fix
data.description: Productize the fix for the recurring iOS PWA bug where the bottom nav dock floats above the screen bottom (and the app rubber-bands like a website). Root-caused + fixed live in ZV's Edge; full writeup in the project folder.
data.nextStep: Confirm with Neil: push+deploy the ZV's Edge fix (currently local-only per his standing no-push convention) and the Reel fix (committed, blocked from prod deploy by the safety classifier -- needs his explicit go-ahead given it's a live app with real friends about to use it). Then pick the first actual product slice per the README's product direction (drop-in shell+diagnostics package is the recommended one, since the diagnostics are what actually solved this bug and most competing blog-post fixes don't ship that part).
data.lastDid: Actually root-caused this for real (2026-07-17), in the Neil AI Hub project. The 2026-07-13 ZV's Edge fix this doc used to describe was incomplete: it recommended pinning html/body with position:fixed;inset:0 and trusting a JS-measured --app-height (visualViewport.height) as "the one number iOS reports truthfully in standalone mode." Both turned out to still be wrong on a real device (an iPhone Pro Max) -- confirmed via a color-coded debug build and on-device diagnostics that WebKit under-reports the standalone viewport height by a fixed ~38pt, and that defeats vh/dvh, visualViewport, innerHeight, AND position:fixed;inset:0 identically, since they all ask WebKit the same underlying question. The actual fix: stop trying to know the exact height at all -- normal document flow + position:sticky for the dock/header (sticky rides the browser's live scroll tracking instead of a reported-and-wrong metric). Rewrote PWA-DOCK-BUG.md to product-quality precision with the full account, then audited every existing app: fixed Neil AI Hub, ZV's Edge (committed locally only, not pushed -- Neil's standing instruction for that repo), and Reel (committed, prod deploy blocked by the safety classifier, needs Neil's go-ahead) with the full sticky-shell fix; added diagnostics + safe-area DOM-probe measurement (without a full rewrite) to Jot and Proof/alcohol-scanner, both of which have a real architectural reason (a native-feel page-per-view model, and a live camera-fill view) to keep an exact-height container; fixed The Gucci Gazette's simpler position:fixed navbar to sticky (found separately uncommitted mid-migration to a new stack, so left uncommitted on purpose). Confirmed Mithoo's Adventure/Gucci Run (games, no dock element) and Rendezvous (no bottom nav at all) aren't affected by this pattern. Also added a pointer in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md and a memory entry so future sessions on any project check the doc before building a PWA shell.

log.text: Found the real root cause (WebKit under-reports the standalone viewport height by a device-specific fixed amount, defeating even position:fixed;inset:0) after the original ZV's Edge fix turned out to be incomplete. Corrected PWA-DOCK-BUG.md to product-quality precision and audited/fixed every existing app against it.

data.name: Jot — all-in-one capture inbox
data.description: One inbox for reminders, Claude ideas, and quick notes instead of three separate iPhone apps — tag as you type, filter, search.
data.nextStep: Neil: confirm the cron-job.org pinger is actually firing (check its execution history for 200s every 1-2min) and/or do the real test -- set a reminder ~5 min out, force-quit the app, confirm the push notification arrives. If that checks out, this fix pass is fully closed out.
data.lastDid: Fable-planner-led hard-fix pass, all live: (1) root-caused the log-page bunching bug to a CSS flexbox quirk (.swipe wrapper's overflow:hidden reset min-size to 0, so a full list got crushed instead of scrolling) — one-line fix, verified before/after with a headless-browser repro; (2) fixed the classifier misreading a bare "7:15" in an evening sentence ("tonight 7:15") as 7:15 AM the next day instead of 7:15 PM — root cause of the duplicate near-identical reminders Neil hit; (3) notification correctness batch: stale timers no longer refire after done/delete/retag, local notified flag only sets on an actual shown notification (so the push backend still gets a shot when permission is denied), guarded the Notification global for plain iOS Safari tabs, subscribe-to-push fires right after permission grant, one-time blocked-notifications toast; (4) new due-time editor — tap a reminder's time line in the Log view to set/change/clear it after capture, previously only settable at capture time; (5) audited the jot-push backend end to end and found the real headline problem: the GitHub Actions cron meant to ping /api/check every 2 min was actually only firing every 3-6 hours in practice (GitHub deprioritizes quiet high-frequency schedules), so closed-app push reminders were arriving hours late this whole time — also fixed check.js to stop permanently marking a reminder notified when zero pushes actually delivered. Backend redeployed, frontend committed and pushed to GitHub Pages.
data.notes: Deliberately buildless (vanilla JS, no React/bundler) since this app is much smaller in scope than the hub. Bottom-anchored composer (thumb zone) instead of top, matching chat-app ergonomics for fast one-handed capture throughout the day. Sync design: app code repo stays public (just code, no data) + a separate private data repo holds the actual JSON via GitHub Contents API — worth retrofitting onto neil-ai-hub's own sync (see its open TODO about the data repo currently being public).

log.text: Finished the push-reliability fix: CRON_SECRET was a Vercel "Sensitive" var (write-only, no dashboard reveal), so rotated it to a new value, redeployed jot-push, and verified the new token works live. Walked Neil through setting up cron-job.org as the real 1-2min pinger (GitHub Actions cadence was unreliable, per earlier finding). Setup complete on Neil's end; awaiting his confirmation the pings are firing / a live end-to-end notification test.

Fable-planner hard-fix pass: root-caused + fixed the log-bunching CSS bug, the tonight-time misparse behind the duplicate-reminder screenshot, a batch of notification-correctness bugs, added a due-time editor, and found/fixed the real reason push reminders were arriving late (GitHub Actions cron running every 3-6h instead of 2min, not a code bug in the backend itself).
Added proper PWA auto-update: checks for a new service worker on foreground and every 60s, auto-reloads once it takes control. No more manual cache-clearing needed for any future update.
Fixed stale content after deploys: service worker now bypasses GitHub Pages' 10-min HTTP cache on fetch/install instead of silently serving old JS/CSS.

Fixed red-bleed-through-cards, text-clipping-on-multiline-notes, and page-scrolls-behind-keyboard bugs Neil caught from phone screenshots. Removed legacy -webkit-overflow-scrolling:touch, locked page scroll via position:fixed+overscroll-behavior:none. Deployed; iOS-specific so needs Neil's phone to confirm.

Built and deployed the real Web Push backend (jot-push on Vercel + GitHub Actions cron every 2 min) to fix the local-timer notification drift Neil hit. Fixed two config bugs (empty jot-data repo, misscoped GitHub token). Server-side verified end-to-end; live phone test pending.

Reworked IA per feedback: capture-only home page (rotating prompt, no visible tag buttons) + separate log page, floating pill nav switcher up top. Added local classify.js for auto-tagging + date/time parsing (no backend) and best-effort local notifications. All-new bright warm visual design distinct from neil-ai-hub. Deployed.

Built v1 of Jot end-to-end: capture UI, tagging, filters/search, sync module, PWA manifest/service worker/icon, and deployed to GitHub Pages at zvcodez.github.io/jot/. Verified all core flows live in-browser.

data.name: Reel — personalized movie/TV app (Letterboxd, but for you)
data.description: A multi-user movie/TV tracking iPhone PWA like Letterboxd, built around personal taste — rate what you've seen and it learns your leanings, then ranks everything unseen against your profile. Friends can see each other's reviews and activity.
data.nextStep: Neil: open https://reel-lac.vercel.app on your iPhone in Safari, sign up for real (Cloudflare's bot-check blocks automated sign-up, so this step needs a human), pick a username, rate a few titles, then Add to Home Screen and confirm it installs standalone with the dock sitting flush (no float/rubber-band). After that: try adding a second account as a friend to test the social flow live. Known follow-ups, none blocking: Clerk is still on its free 'Development mode' instance (small watermark on auth pages — fine for personal/friends use, would need a paid plan + custom domain to remove); createRouteMatcher-based route protection is deprecated in this Clerk version (still functional, Clerk recommends migrating to per-route auth checks eventually); parked feature ideas below are all still open.
data.lastDid: Took this the rest of the way to a deployed, fully-featured app across Phases 2-6 in one continuous session. Phase 2: provisioned Neon Postgres via Vercel Marketplace, migrated the schema, built the rate/review/rewatch UI (stars + preset tags + free text + editable date-watched) and migrated watchlist from localStorage to the DB — caught a real date-timezone bug (new Date('YYYY-MM-DD') parsing as UTC shifted 'today' back a day in US timezones) and fixed it by switching to a plain SQL date column. Phase 3: wired up Clerk (Marketplace install, themed to match Reel's own dark/light palette instead of default styling), username-based onboarding, every API route now resolves the real signed-in user — hit and fixed a genuine breaking change along the way (Next 16 renamed middleware.ts to proxy.ts, exactly the kind of thing this project's own AGENTS.md warns about). Phase 4: friends — username search, requests, and a merged activity feed of friends' reviews; verified the trickier logic (reverse-request auto-accept, unique-constraint enforcement, the activity join) directly against the DB since two real signed-up accounts can't be scripted past Clerk's bot-check. Phase 5: ported the taste engine to live watch_log data and built Tonight's Pick (director-enriched top-5 reroll); validated the actual scoring pipeline against real TMDB data with seeded ratings before trusting it. Phase 6: deployed to Vercel (zv-edge/reel) — caught one real production-only gap (NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_SIGN_IN_URL/SIGN_UP_URL only ever existed in .env.local, never pushed to Vercel's env store, so production was silently falling back to Clerk's generic hosted sign-in page instead of Reel's own themed one) and fixed it before calling it done.
data.notes:
• Log, rate and review the movies/shows you watch, like Letterboxd — but the whole app bends around your taste
• Real taste engine live now: genre/mood/decade/director weights computed from your actual ratings, Tonight's Pick uses it for a single tailored suggestion with reroll
• Real accounts (Clerk) + friends (username search, requests, merged activity feed of what friends watched/rated)
• Visual identity: cinematic dark (near-black, poster art forward, italic serif wordmark, single crimson accent) with a warm-cream light mode alternate, manual toggle persisted locally — Clerk's own auth UI themed to match, not default styling
• Real installable iPhone PWA (manifest + service worker + icons), Next.js-native, dock-bug-safe shell
• Data model: watch_log (one row per watch instance — doubles as reviews AND rewatch tracking) + watchlist + friend_requests (accepted row IS the friendship), all in Postgres via Drizzle/Neon
• All 6 planned phases shipped: TMDB browse/search/detail → Postgres persistence → Clerk auth → friends/social → taste engine/Tonight's Pick → deployed to Vercel
• Parked feature ideas for a future pass: Year in Film recap, shareable rating card, custom lists/collections, direct friend recommendations, shared-taste overlap, Letterboxd CSV import, offline-queued reviews

log.text: Shipped Phases 2 through 6 in one session and deployed to https://reel-lac.vercel.app: real Postgres persistence (ratings/reviews/rewatch tracking, watchlist), Clerk accounts with themed sign-in/sign-up, a friends/social layer with an activity feed, a working taste engine powering Tonight's Pick, and production deployment. Fixed a real timezone date bug, a Next 16 middleware->proxy rename, and a production-only env var gap that was silently sending sign-in through Clerk's generic hosted page instead of Reel's own. Next: Neil needs to do the actual first sign-up himself (Cloudflare bot-check blocks scripted sign-up) and Add to Home Screen on his phone to close the loop.

Scope grew to multi-user with a friends/social layer, so rebuilt Reel from the static PWA prototype into a real Next.js app (~/Claude/reel), matching rendezvous's stack (App Router/TS/Tailwind v4/shadcn/Drizzle+Neon). Shipped Phase 1: cinematic dark/light PWA shell (carried forward the dock-bug-safe invariants, caught and fixed a real SW reload-loop bug), plus live TMDB integration — real search, genre-filterable Movies/TV browse, and a detail page with cast/crew, trailers, where-to-watch, and similar titles, all verified end-to-end with real data. Getting the TMDB key was the real time-sink: signup 403'd repeatedly across wifi/LTE/VPN due to a known TMDB bug blocking Gmail addresses specifically, fixed with a non-Gmail signup email.

Locked the visual direction (cinematic dark + light/dark toggle) via 4 side-by-side mockups, then rebuilt Reel as a real installable PWA in that identity: manifest, self-updating service worker, generated icons, iOS meta tags. Applied the bulletproof PWA-shell pattern from the dock-issue project so the bottom tab bar can't hit the same floating-dock bug Neil already fixed once in ZV's Edge and Jot — verified with Playwright + real Chrome under iPhone 14 emulation (exact viewport fill, no overflow, header/dock static through inner scroll). Also fixed a latent bug (director field always undefined due to an off-by-one array index) surfaced while screenshotting the Taste tab.

Built and browser-verified a working v1 ('Reel'): curated offline library + a genuine taste engine that learns from your star ratings and ranks unseen titles by profile match, each with a match % and a plain-English reason. Includes mood/type filters, a taste-profile visualizer, browse and watchlist. Single self-contained HTML file — double-click to run, ratings persist locally. Git repo initialized.

data.name: Rally — going-out app (nights at the bars with mates)
data.description: The live, during-the-night companion for going out with your friends: one shared board showing who's in, where everyone is, and where you're heading next — instead of 40 unread group texts.
data.nextStep: Neil: read ~/Claude/going-out/BRIEF.md and try the prototype (index.html) — then confirm the hook. Is the live 'crew board' the right wedge, or did you picture discovery (finding the bar) or a game layer? (See the 3 open questions at the end of the brief.)
data.lastDid: Since the hook wasn't nailed yet, I wrote a product brief that PICKS one: the app is the live during-the-night crew board (who's in · where you are · where next), deliberately NOT another photos-after app (that's the separate Nights Out idea). Then built a working prototype 'Rally' and verified it: create tonight, add your crew, per-person live status (In/Maybe/On my way/Done), propose + vote venues with a computed 'Next stop', and a live activity feed. Parked the discovery/game/rounds layers as v2.
data.notes:
• The wedge: the group chat is bad at the one thing that matters in the moment — who's actually out, where is everyone, where next. This app is that one glanceable screen.
• HOOK PICKED (v1): the live crew board — statuses, venue voting → 'Next stop', activity pulse. Useful the first night, needs no seed content, and it's the thing the group chat can't do.
• Deliberately NOT the memories app — this is the LIVE companion; it could hand off to [[idea-nights-out]] at the end of the night.
• Parked for v2+: bar discovery/deals (needs a places API), a social game/dares layer, rounds/split-the-tab.
• BUILT: working single-device prototype + full product brief (BRIEF.md). Next: confirm the hook with Neil, then real multi-device sync.

log.text: Turned the fuzzy 'something for going out' idea into a decision: wrote a product brief that picks the hook (the live during-the-night crew board) over the alternatives (discovery / game / rounds), with a comparison table and open questions. Then built and browser-verified a working prototype 'Rally': tonight's board with per-person live status, venue proposals + voting that compute a 'Next stop', and an activity feed. Single-file HTML, git repo initialized. Needs Neil to confirm the hook, then real multi-device sync.

data.name: Vibe DJ — Spotify vibe-based queue app
data.description: An app where you 'DJ' your own Spotify by feel — pick a vibe and it auto-builds a queue that flows, with recommendations that match the mood; the dream version lets you just talk to it via a Claude + Spotify connector.
data.nextStep: Working and personalized end-to-end. Real remaining limit: Spotify killed audio-features, recommendations AND artist genres for new apps, so true open-ended mood recommendations across his taste need the Claude+Spotify connector version (v2 dream) rather than Spotify data. Options when he wants more: (1) build that talk-to-it v2; (2) host on HTTPS so it runs on his phone off the laptop server; (3) add saved-tracks/playlist sources. Run: cd ~/Claude && python3 -m http.server 5599 --bind 127.0.0.1 then http://127.0.0.1:5599/spotify-dj/index.html .
data.lastDid: Made Vibe DJ genuinely personalized to Neils real listening (he pushed back that the mood tiles felt random). Rebuilt the connected experience around Spotify data that still works on a new app: Your rotation (On repeat 4wk / Recently played / Your top 6mo / All-time from /me/top + recently-played) and Your top artists (tap Drake / AP Dhillon / Diljit etc to build a set from their catalogue + his own tracks by them), plus More-like-this that extends the current song with more from the same artist. Discovered along the way that Spotify also returns EMPTY artist genres on this tier (not just audio-features/recommendations), so pivoted from genre chips to artist chips. Verified live: picked AP Dhillon -> real 10-track set on his Echo with album art, More-like-this added his collabs, clean console.
data.notes:
• 'DJ from the app': pick a vibe, it builds and plays a set; skip/queue/shift on the fly
• Vibe-based: choose a mood and it queues tracks that match, ordered to flow
• Dream version: Claude + a Spotify connector so you just talk to it ('keep this energy', 'something slower next') and it manages the queue — SETUP.md sketches this
• BUILT: a fully interactive DEMO (real vibe engine + queue + recommendations; simulated playback + local library). The engine's energy/valence fields ARE Spotify's audio-feature names, so it swaps in directly.
• ONE BLOCKER only Neil can clear: a Spotify Developer app (free) + Premium (needed to control playback). Steps in SETUP.md.
• Nice tie-in with the going-out / nights ecosystem

log.text: Repersonalized to real listening: Your rotation + top-artist sets + more-like-this, all from live Spotify data. Verified on his account (AP Dhillon set on the Echo).
Connected to Spotify and confirmed live: vibe -> real set -> real playback on his Echo Dot with album art + true playback state. Fully working.

Built real Spotify integration (PKCE auth, live search, real playback via Web Playback SDK + Connect). Worked around Spotify's 2024 removal of audio-features/recommendations by keeping the vibe engine local. Preview path verified; live path awaits Neil's Client ID + Premium.

Built and browser-verified a working DEMO ('Vibe DJ'): 8 vibe tiles that each auto-build a ~12-track set ordered to flow, simulated now-playing + transport, recommend-one-more, a searchable queue library, and conversational vibe-shift controls. The scoring runs on energy/valence — Spotify's own audio-feature fields — so it ports straight to the real API. Also wrote SETUP.md documenting the exact real-Spotify wiring (PKCE auth, /recommendations with target features, Web Playback SDK vs Connect, and the Claude-connector 'just talk to it' version). Blocked only on Neil creating a Spotify dev app + Premium; demo is playable now. Git repo initialized.

data.name: Vow Bets — wedding guest wagers (with Amar)
data.description: A wedding-industry app (idea with Amar) that lets guests place friendly play-money bets on how the day unfolds — 'will the bride cry?', 'speech over 5 min?' — a shared game with a live leaderboard that makes the celebration more fun.
data.nextStep: Neil: try ~/Claude/wedding-wagers/index.html (place a bet, flip on Host mode, resolve it). Then loop in Amar on which half he wants to own. Product call to lock: keep v1 play-money only (recommended — no gambling regulation). Build step: real multi-device rooms (join by code/QR).
data.lastDid: Built a working v1 prototype 'Vow Bets' and verified the full loop end-to-end in-browser: create the book (couple/date/host) which seeds 8 classic wedding prop bets with decimal odds, add guests with 1000 play chips each, 'pass the phone' to switch bettor, place bets with live payout preview (chips deduct), and Host mode → resolve a market → winners auto-paid. Confirmed: 100 chips @ 1.40× took the balance 1000 → 900 → 1040 on a win. Also supports adding custom prop bets, a leaderboard, and a My Bets view. Deliberately play-money only (no gambling/regulation).
data.notes:
• Idea co-owned with Amar — for the wedding industry
• Guests place bets on outcomes during the day to make it more entertaining; live leaderboard; results revealed as things happen
• Example markets (8 seeded): bride cries, best man's speech over 5 min, who cries first, first dance flawless/fumble, couple late, bouquet catcher, surprise announcement, total speech time
• Couple/organiser sets up custom prop bets; guests would join via code/QR at the reception (multi-device is the next build)
• KEY DECISION — money model (v1 answered: PLAY-MONEY):
  – Play-money / chips = pure fun, bragging rights, NO gambling regulation → this is what's built
  – Real-money = actual gambling, heavily regulated, needs licensing — separate much-bigger later step, NOT a v1 toggle
• Monetisation (non-gambling): couples pay to host, premium market packs, sponsor prizes
• BUILT: working play-money prototype, full bet→resolve→payout loop verified. Next: real multi-device rooms; talk to Amar.

log.text: Built and browser-verified a working v1 ('Vow Bets', play-money): setup seeds 8 classic wedding prop bets with odds; add guests with 1000 chips; pass-the-phone bettor switch; place bets with live payout preview; Host mode resolves a market and auto-pays winners. Verified the full loop (100 @ 1.40x → 1000→900→1040 on a win). Custom markets, leaderboard, My Bets all work. Single-file HTML, git repo initialized. Deliberately play-money to avoid gambling regulation. Next: multi-device rooms + loop in Amar.

data.name: The Gucci Gazette
data.description: A shared photo album app for Gucci — snap a photo, get an AI caption, send it to Diksha (or vice versa) with a push notification; every photo is kept in a cozy-scrapbook-style shared feed organized by month.
data.nextStep: Neil adds ANTHROPIC_API_KEY via 'vercel env add' (the one manual step for AI captions to work), then both phones do the PIN + ntfy setup from HANDOFF.md.
data.lastDid: Discovered an earlier, unfinished build (Gucci — Moments) already existed and replaced it end to end in place: cozy-scrapbook design (polaroid cards, tap-to-expand full photo), bidirectional sending, Vercel Blob photo storage, Claude-vision AI captions, ntfy push, shared-PIN access. Deployed to the same Vercel project/URL.
data.notes:
• Open the app and instantly take a photo or video of Gucci
• Everything saves automatically into his personal gallery
• Organized into albums (e.g. by date, location, or mood/activity)
• Built entirely around him — his name, his photos, his vibe
• A dedicated place to collect every cute moment, all in one spot

data.name: Macro Tracker
data.description: A simple app for logging food and tracking macros (calories, protein, carbs, fat) to support gym goals.
data.notes:
• Log meals throughout the day and see running macro totals vs. daily targets
• Set custom goals (e.g. high protein for muscle gain, calorie deficit for cutting)
• Quick-add common foods or search a nutrition database
• Daily/weekly summary so you can see patterns over time
• Gym-focused: built around hitting protein targets, not just calorie counting

data.name: Nights Out memories app
data.description: A shared app for friends to upload photos from a night out, compiled into a per-night gallery to look back on.
data.notes:
• Each night out gets its own album — photos from everyone in the group land in one place
• Friends can add pics from their phones during or after the night
• Browse back through past nights like a timeline of memories
• Simple and social — no faff, just upload and view
• Could include the date, venue, or a title for each night

data.name: Proof — Alcohol Scanner & Tracker
data.description: Point your phone at any beer or bottle to instantly see its nutrition info, health score, and reviews — and keep a log of everything you've scanned.
data.nextStep: Neil to retest: scan the Good & Gather bottle again, then try quickly swapping between two different bottles to confirm results never mix up.
data.lastDid: Fixed a race-condition bug from live testing: scanning a Good & Gather water bottle showed a stale Poland Spring result instead. Root cause was the camera continuing to decode while the Open Food Facts lookup was in flight, so an older scan's network response could resolve after a newer one and overwrite the result screen. Now the camera stops the instant a read is confirmed (before the lookup starts), plus a scan-token guard discards any late-arriving stale response. Bumped cache-busting version (v=2->v=3) and the service worker cache name so the fix actually reaches the phone instead of being served from cache. Deployed to production.
data.notes:
• Scan a beer or spirit (barcode or photo of the label) and instantly get nutritional info: calories, sugar, carbs, ABV
• Personal inventory — everything you scan gets logged so you have a history of what you've tried
• Health ranking: scores each drink based on how healthy (or not) it is — e.g. low-cal beers rank higher than sugary cocktails
• Review ranking: pulls in ratings/reviews from the web so you can see how popular or well-regarded a drink is
• Great use case: standing in the liquor store comparing two bottles — scan both and see which scores better on health and reviews

data.name: Claude Code chunk-done push notifications
data.description: Get a phone push when a Claude Code session finishes a chunk of work, so I can step away from the desk and know exactly when (and which terminal) to come back to.
data.notes:
• A Stop hook already pings my iPhone via ntfy.sh when a session ends a chunk — always on, mute from the phone
• THE PROBLEM TO SOLVE: every open session pings me, and they all look the same — I got 'come back' alerts for the AI hub while I was actually working in zvs-edge, with no way to tell sessions apart
• Want the alert to show the terminal/chat name (the title the terminal auto-makes from my first message), not just the project folder
• Catch: the hook can't read the terminal tab title — it only gets the folder + session id; need another way to label each session (likely capture its first prompt)
• Ideal end state: only the session(s) I care about ping me, each clearly labeled, one mute switch on the phone

data.name: Mithoo's Adventure
data.description: A Flappy Bird–style game made to honor Mithoo, my pet bird.
data.nextStep: Play a commute run on the iPhone; confirm the day/night sky is wanted back (was removed Jul 11) and tune berry frequency/medal tiers to taste.
data.lastDid: Big commute-friendly upgrade pack: cache-first offline loading, auto-pause on app switch with 3-2-1 resume, post-crash tap lockout, bronze/silver/gold medals + New Best confetti, cosmetic unlock ladder (crown/scarf/shades/golden trail), score-based day-to-sunset-to-night sky (note: reintroduces the sky fade removed Jul 11), +2 berry collectibles, daily best score, and spacebar/rotation bug fixes. Smoke-tested headless, pushed live.
data.notes:
• Tap to flap Mithoo through gaps between obstacles
• Built as a little tribute to my bird — use his real colors/look for the character
• Simple one-tap gameplay; score = obstacles passed, best score saved
• Fun touches: a chirp sound on each flap, themed backgrounds
• Can be a lightweight web/phone game — no app store needed

log.text: Upgrade pack shipped: offline-first SW, auto-pause/resume countdown, death tap lockout, medals + new-best confetti, 4-tier cosmetic unlocks, day/night sky by score, berry collectibles (+2), daily best, input/rotation fixes.

Personalized the game for Diksha's late bird Mithoo: real lovebird coloring, flower-pot obstacles you can fly behind, and background cameos (her, her sister Pragya, dog Gucci, their house). Then added a fun/polish pass: particle effects, butterflies, sunset sky shift, milestone messages, a cosmetic crown unlock, lifetime stats, ambient sound + mute, and score sharing. Found and fixed a few real bugs along the way via headless-browser testing rather than just eyeballing the code.

Shipped v1: playable flappy-style PWA installable on iPhone home screen, fully offline. Fixed first-play pipe spawn bug, stale score display bug, and a stale service-worker cache bug. Reskinned bird to Mithoo's real coloring and pipes to flower pots; added a decorative background scene (house + two figures) as a tribute touch.
UI cleanup + visual polish pass: dropped flights/flowers stat, sunny-only sky, rounder people, cream-colored dog. Pushed to live site.

data.name: Subway Surfers–style runner (for my girlfriend)
data.description: An endless runner game my girlfriend can play offline on her way to work.
data.nextStep: Have Diksha (or Neil) play it on the actual iPhone and confirm the dizziness is gone in real hand-held play. Tunable knobs are documented at the top of game.js and in HANDOFF.md if it still needs adjusting.
data.lastDid: Diagnosed why the road felt dizzying/flat (a single power-curve hack instead of real perspective) and rewrote the whole renderer: true 1/depth perspective, segmented rumble-strip road, gentle procedural curves/hills, real parallax (skyline/buildings/road at different speeds), speed-coupled clouds, and a world lean/bank on lane changes. Verified in-browser (no errors across long fast-forwarded runs), redeployed to production.
data.notes:
• Swipe to dodge obstacles and grab coins; it speeds up as you go
• Must work fully offline (no signal on the commute)
• Personalize it for her — character, theme, maybe some inside jokes
• Quick pick-up-and-play sessions; high scores saved on the phone
• Simple touch controls, designed for holding the phone upright

data.name: AI beginner journey newsletter
data.description: A newsletter sharing my beginner AI journey with Claude — and hopefully making some money from it.
data.notes:
• Write articles about learning AI as a beginner, specifically using Claude
• Use Claude to help brainstorm, draft, and edit each post
• Pick a platform with built-in subscriptions (like Substack or beehiiv)
• Make money later through paid subscribers or sponsors once there's an audience
• Share real projects (like this hub) as honest behind-the-scenes case studies

data.name: Algo trading with Claude
data.description: Learning-focused algo trading project: Python pipeline (data -> strategy -> backtest -> paper trade) connected to an Alpaca paper trading account.
data.nextStep: Test a different strategy family (e.g. trend-following with a volatility filter, or plain buy-and-hold vs. mean reversion) for comparison. Longer term: only after a strategy holds up over real paper-trading time, consider a scheduled Claude Code routine to run it automatically.
data.lastDid: Set up the project (Python venv, git repo) and connected it to a real Alpaca paper trading account ($100k fake money) -- confirmed the connection and placed a real test order end-to-end. Built a data/strategy/backtest pipeline and tested a cross-sectional momentum rotation strategy (SPY/QQQ/IWM/EFA/EEM/TLT/GLD, monthly rebalance) over 19 years of history. Honest result: it underperformed plain buy-and-hold SPY overall (7.98% CAGR vs 10.70%, worse Sharpe) despite much smaller drawdowns in real crashes (2008, 2022) -- it rarely actually held SPY (2.6% of months) and bled value chasing weaker assets (gold/small-cap/EM/bonds) during the long grinding bull years. Good first lesson in why rigorous backtesting matters.
data.notes:
• Explore 'algo trading' — automated, rule-based buy/sell strategies
• Use Claude and Claude Code to research, prototype, and test ideas
• Start simple and paper-trade (fake money) first to see if it actually works
• Learn the basics: where to get data, indicators, and managing risk
• Keep it as a learning experiment early on — real trading carries real risk
• Alpaca free data plan caps historical bars at ~6 years -- use yfinance for deep backtesting research, Alpaca for the actual paper account/execution

log.text: First real build session. Researched how people are actually using Claude Code for trading right now (Alpaca's official MCP server, Robinhood's new Agentic Trading, Claude Code 'Routines' running scheduled research/trade/journal loops) and talked through the fundamentals -- profit vs. beating a benchmark, what algo trading actually means, options/futures risk. Scaffolded the project, connected to a real Alpaca paper account, placed a test order, then built a data -> strategy -> backtest pipeline and ran a first real strategy (cross-sectional momentum rotation across major ETFs). Result: it lost to plain buy-and-hold SPY over 19 years despite better crash protection -- diagnosed exactly why (it almost never actually held SPY, kept rotating into laggards).

data.name: ZV's Edge
data.description: An AI sports betting app for my iPhone that helps spot good bets to make. Now fully focused on MLB (FIFA/Kalshi retired from the app, kept in the codebase for reuse).
data.nextStep: Nothing to do until games resume (~Jul 17 after the All-Star break) — the 3pm ET pull then locks the first always-on Play of the Day. To see the new app icon, delete + re-add the app to the home screen. Credits: 40 left, budgeted to the Aug 1 reset.
data.lastDid: Big overhaul, all deployed live: fixed a real credit leak (app opens no longer spend odds credits; snapshot persists across restarts; All-Star-break days cost 0), made the model always produce one Play of the Day (best win-probability at standard odds), backtested the policy (would have gone 44-29, 60.3%, one pick/day since May 1) and loosened the price floor to -200 based on that evidence. New glassy design with light/dark mode toggle, win-probability ring, streak flame, lock countdown, and a new ZVS EDGE app icon. Fixed the raised-dock iOS PWA bug (documented in the dock-issue project).
data.notes: Live and feature-rich (picks, locked POTD + switch flow, live tracker, push). Now backed up to GitHub.

log.text: 2026-07-13/14 session: credit-leak fix (page loads now free; snapshot persisted; break days skipped), always-a-play POTD with a 60.3% backtested one-pick-a-day policy (price floor -130 -> -200 on that evidence), glass redesign v3 with light mode + new ZVS EDGE icon, app-shell scrolling fix (no more whole-page rubber-band), and the raised-dock iOS PWA bug root-caused + fixed (writeup in ~/Claude/dock-issue). Backend on Railway + frontend on Vercel both deployed and verified; live URL confirmed: https://zvs-edge.vercel.app

Visual refresh, per your request: swapped every emoji (bolt, bell, bar chart, baseball, etc.) for a consistent hand-drawn icon set, and added real depth to the design — soft shadows, subtle gradients on cards and the headline Play-of-the-Day card, a glowing gold nav highlight, smoother-looking numbers. Same colors/layout/meaning as before, just more polished and less flat. Committed locally; not pushed or deployed yet, waiting on your look before shipping it.

Retired FIFA and Kalshi from the live app (Neil's call, once MLB's trial period showed real results) — their code and bet history stay in the project for possible reuse on future sports, just not shown anymore. Built MLB its own daily rhythm: a reliable 3pm ET pull (instead of a rolling clock that had drifted to firing after games already started), which locks in the Play of the Day and 3 Yankees-focused picks and tracks them to a real win/loss and profit/loss. Caught a real database bug during testing that would have broken the live app on deploy, fixed it before shipping. Deployed everywhere.

Finished building player-prop bets (Total Bases, Hits, Home Runs, Strikeouts) and connected them into the same picks list the app already uses for Play of the Day, Next Best, and the Yankees section — individual-player bets now compete alongside team bets everywhere. Strikeout bets turned out to be the strongest signal found in the whole model so far. Also caught a real problem: the monthly odds-data allowance was on pace to run out about two weeks before it resets, so slowed down how often the app checks for fresh odds to make it last.

MLB model: added park factors (some stadiums inflate/suppress scoring for both teams) after last session's test found the model had basically no real predictive edge without them. Caught and fixed a sneaky data bug where a few ballparks got renamed by sponsors mid-season, which was splitting one park's real history into two thin, unreliable pieces. Locked in the best settings from testing. Changed the picks logic per Neil's request: it now only recommends bets it's genuinely more than 50% confident will win, at normal (not longshot) odds, ranked by confidence rather than payout size -- and added a small Yankees-focused section (3 strongest Yankees bets). Tested against today's real slate: almost nothing cleared the new, stricter bar, which is the intended effect of being pickier. Committed locally, not yet pushed.

Fixed two real bugs found while explaining tonight's USA-Belgium Under 2.5 POTD to Neil: a stale qualifies flag (locked POTD re-priced live could keep qualifies=true even after its edge dropped below the 5% bar -- now a property derived straight from edge) and backwards totals rationale text (the "combined goals" bullet always claimed to support whichever side was picked instead of comparing the number to the line; also fixed the per-team hit-rate count to use the pick's actual line instead of a hardcoded 2.5, which was silently wrong for any alternate-line pick). Audited the rest of the pick/board/settlement/tracker pipeline for the same class of bug -- clean. Deployed to Railway, pushed to GitHub.

Added a closest-lean POTD fallback: when nothing clears 5% edge, shows the highest-edge pick anyway, flagged red instead of a blank card, and it's tracked in the model's record too (Neil's call). Also discussed and decided to keep POTD's odds unconstrained by price, unlike Next Best/parlays. Deployed to Railway + Vercel.

Added a real app icon (gold bolt + ZV/EDGE wordmark). Added an odds-range filter for Next Best Plays + a normal parlay; added a second "jackpot" parlay category (2 legs, +400+, floor-dropped true-prob gate). Built end-to-end parlay tracking (auto-logged, model-only, no placed toggle, graded on settlement, shown in Tracker). Deployed to Railway + Vercel.

Follow-up polish: removed the confidence tooltip icon (kept the glossary entry). Fixed the bottom nav visibly jumping on tab switch -- iOS Safari detaches position:fixed elements from the real viewport bottom when its chrome bar animates; switched .app to a flex column with .nav as position:sticky instead, which is immune to that. Confirmed fixed on Neil's phone.

Design/functionality session: renamed confidence tier 'Borderline'->'Lean' + added tooltip/glossary entry; built swipe-down pull-to-refresh; built the two missing push alert types (kickoff reminder ~30min pre-match, big line move >=4pt shift) and confirmed live delivery works on Neil's phone. Deployed both services.

Parlay/next-best 'disappeared' was a frontend empty-state bug, not data loss. Backend correctly declined to force a parlay (joint prob ~4% < 10% floor) or dark horse (edge < 10%). Home now always shows those sections with an explanation when empty. Deployed.

Model card +P&L, settlement void/unmatched safety nets (audit #1+#2), and POTD validity confirmed. Clarified Neil's odds confusion: app's +260 = Match Result (90-min win); FanDuel's +144 he saw = To Qualify (incl ET/pens) — different markets. Odds are fetched on-demand (fresh), board hardens ~30min pre-kickoff. Open product decision: knockout market choice (Match Result vs To Qualify).

Fixed penalty-shootout score ingestion (fullTime included pen kicks -> 1-1 game read as 4-5). Verified live: feed now 1-1, Germany record 2-1-1. Audited the rest of the settlement path; flagged abandoned-match void gap + team-name join failures as the next silent-failure risks.

Shipped the whole-board lock + tracker fixes to prod. Root-caused the recurring data wipe: no Railway volume — attached one at /data so the DB persists. Caught that neither Railway nor Vercel auto-deployed the last push; deployed both by CLI. Model record seeded 1-0 from the Germany/Paraguay BTTS hit (logged placed=0, settled off the real results feed).

Backend session: folded auto-settlement into run_alert_check so finished bets grade automatically (it was stuck behind ENABLE_SCHEDULER, off in prod to save odds credits). Settlement runs first + unconditionally on the free results feed so the Tracker P&L stays current; added a 'bet settled' push for placed bets reusing tracker.bet_profit_units; and made settle_pending short-circuit when nothing's pending. Compile + smoke tested, pushed to main. Open question to confirm: ENABLE_ALERTS must be on in Railway for the loop to run. Next up: Neil's Home page changes.

Rewrote the stale 'scaffold only' README to reflect the live feature-complete app (model, picks, push, tracker, deploy) and pushed it. Scoped the next phase with Neil: work ONLY on Home + Tracker, ignore Lines/Bracket/Analysis. Backend audit of the live Railway service found three real gaps that matter for those two pages: auto-settlement isn't running (settle job gated behind disabled scheduler), scheduled odds ticks are off (odds refresh on app-open only), and CLV is a hard stub (NotImplementedError, blocked on OddsPapi API docs). Agreed priority: fix settlement first (fold into the alert loop that's already on), then a Home/Tracker design pass using Neil's feedback.

Created the private GitHub repo zvcodez/zvs-edge and pushed main. The remote was already wired over SSH, just needed the empty repo on GitHub's side — code is now versioned and backed up off the machine.

Major post-launch session. Renamed to a clean domain (zvs-edge.vercel.app). Fixed the things Neil flagged: stale odds now refresh on load, picks scoped to TODAY's slate (no more tomorrow's game as POTD), FanDuel price leads with a DraftKings 'better line' flag, concrete bet-specific 'why' + parlay reasoning, bankroll asks on first launch ($400), freshness stamp, bracket explainer. Built Web Push end-to-end (VAPID + 5 toggles + bet-aware alerts). Locked the Play of the Day so it never auto-changes; added an in-app Update section that suggests a stronger play (did-you-place → switch, or cash-out-vs-ride) with why-better bullets and a switch history; and a live in-play tracker showing how close the bet is to hitting. Backend on Railway, frontend on Vercel — all deployed and verified.

Launched the app live on the internet — backend on Railway, frontend on Vercel, wired together. Hit and fixed a few hosting snags (Python version, packaging). The live app now pulls real World Cup picks from the model (today’s Play of the Day: Germany vs Paraguay, Both Teams To Score). Next: add to iPhone home screen + notifications.

Got everything ready to put the app online — set up the hosting files and wrote a step-by-step guide for launching it. Still just the basic skeleton at this point: no betting features built yet, and no GitHub repo connected.
Started the project — an AI sports betting app for my iPhone to help spot good bets. Scaffolded the app structure to build on.

data.name: Neil AI Hub
data.description: My personal dashboard that keeps track of all my AI projects in one place.
data.lastDid: Fixed several reported bugs in one session: Reel wasn't showing in Neil's Apps (stale localStorage that only ever self-healed when empty — now always re-checks committed data), apps weren't alphabetical, app links opened in an in-app Safari sheet instead of handing off, and rebuilt Home as a stats-forward "mission control" layout. The bottom dock float took three rounds to actually fix: round 1 restructured the CSS shell but also added a JS-measured --app-height var on the assumption visualViewport.height is trustworthy in standalone iOS -- it isn't (same WebKit bug as 100vh/100dvh) -- so that override was the actual bug, confirmed via a color-coded debug build on Neil's real device. Fixed by dropping the JS override and trusting the CSS position:fixed;inset:0 pin alone. Added a visible build stamp + live viewport/safe-area diagnostics in the sync panel so future rounds start from real device numbers instead of guessing from screenshots.
data.nextStep: Neil: force-quit and reopen the Hub twice, confirm build v58 in the sync panel, and check the bottom dock sits flush with no dead strip below it. If it's still wrong, screenshot the sync panel's diagnostics readout first (standalone/innerHeight/vv height/safe-top/safe-bottom) before any more CSS changes — guessing without device numbers is what cost two earlier rounds. Once confirmed, Reel/alphabetical-order/link-handoff/Home redesign from the same session are done and don't need re-checking.
data.notes: One place to keep an eye on all my AI projects.

log.text: Fixed Reel-missing/alphabetical-order/link-handoff bugs, rebuilt Home as "mission control", and after three rounds found the real dock-float root cause: a JS-measured --app-height var was overriding the already-correct CSS pin with a value from a viewport API (visualViewport.height) that turns out to be just as unreliable in standalone iOS as 100vh/100dvh. Added a build stamp + on-device diagnostics readout in the sync panel so this doesn't require guessing blind again.

Diagnosed the 'everything gone on phone' issue: iOS evicts localStorage (taking the sync token with it). Added self-heal bootstrap in sync.js — empty stores now reseed from the site's own deployed data files on startup, so the app never opens blank again. SW cache bumped to v53.
Shipped Neil's Apps launcher on Home — grid of chips derived from projects' liveUrl fields, opens each app's live deployment in a new browser tab; stays current automatically as liveUrls change.

Fixed a reported bug: on iOS standalone-mode cold start (fresh install/update/reload), the bottom nav floated above the home indicator instead of sitting flush, even though the existing CSS safe-area-inset-bottom/dvh handling was already correct. Root cause: iOS can report stale env()/dvh values on cold start until a resize/scroll/orientation event fires, and nothing was triggering that recalc. Added forceSafeAreaRecalc() near the top of main.js — toggles the viewport meta's viewport-fit (cover→auto→cover) to force iOS to re-derive env() values, plus dispatches a synthetic resize event for dvh. Runs immediately on load, again at 100/500/1000ms (env() can populate late), and on orientationchange. Bumped sw cache to v51 so the fix actually reaches devices with the old main.js cached. Pushed live; not yet verified on a physical device — Neil to test via fresh reinstall.

Shipped and deployed redesign v2 (same day, second pass — v1 still felt “low-budget, like Notion”). Added an app-wide identity layer: self-hosted Sora as the display face (headlines/titles/stat numbers), a low-opacity ambient gradient wash behind the whole shell instead of just Home, an inline SVG brand mark replacing the flat “NP” square, and StatTile/Sparkline primitives for a mission-control stat feel. Rebuilt Projects: cards simplified to name + description + launch button (editing moved into detail), a new alphabetical All Apps list, and the old centered detail modal replaced with a full drill-in screen the clicked card morphs into via the View Transitions API. Iterated on board layout with Neil before landing: he rejected a single-page stacked-sections layout (didn't want to scroll past all of Building to reach Live), so kept the 3-column kanban but fixed a real bug in it — stage-cards was flex-direction:row, so Building's 11 cards rendered ~3400px wide in one row; now cards stack downward per column with independent capped scroll on desktop. Also added an optional `version` tag and moved the live URL to right after the description in detail, per Neil's explicit ask. Iterated with two rounds of interactive Artifact mockups (font choice, then full Projects vision) before writing any real code. Syntax-checked and smoke-tested locally (serve.sh + curl); could not visually verify in a real browser this session (no browser tool available) — Neil still needs to eyeball it on Mac + phone. sw cache v42.

Kicked off the visual redesign Neil asked for. Mocked up 4 style directions (ambient glass / command-center / minimal editorial / neumorphic) as a side-by-side artifact so he could pick before any code changed — he chose ambient glass for a new Home tab, but wanted regular tabs to stay plain white/black per theme with just light glass touches (soft card glow, gradient buttons, shared blue/violet/amber/teal accent trio) rather than full glass everywhere. Shipped: a new Home tab (time-aware greeting, live counts, quick-select into Projects/Career/Business, theme-adaptive blob background); a new Business tab (Overview roll-up + Ventures pipeline, store key business-ventures, now synced) for side-venture ideas separate from the Projects pipeline; a combined Growth tab folding Timeline/Skills/Glossary/Resources into one set of sub-tabs. Nav reordered: sidebar is Home/Projects/Career/Business/Growth, mobile bottom bar is Projects/Career/Home(center)/Business/Growth. Verified in headless Chrome across light/dark and desktop/mobile widths — no console errors, no overflow bugs. sw cache v41. Neil reviewed it locally on his Mac and phone (over LAN, after a stale service-worker-on-5173 snag from an unrelated Vite project) and approved it — deployed live to GitHub Pages. He mentioned there's more redesign/IA discussion still to come, no specifics yet.

Answered where the new Excel tracker lives by adding a green Tracker button to the Applications toolbar. Since a browser cannot open local files directly, extended the existing ClaudeCodeOpener.app (originally built for the claudecode:// Open in Claude Code button) to also handle a neilhub:// scheme; neilhub://open-tracker runs open -R on the tracker file to reveal+select it in Finder. Chose reveal over open deliberately: this Mac has no Excel or Numbers installed (checked via mdfind), so a plain open would silently fail with zero feedback, whereas Finder reveal always works and spacebar Quick Look previews the xlsx with no app needed. Verified end to end: rebuilt/registered the app, triggered the scheme from Terminal and from an actual button click in the browser, confirmed Finder opened to Downloads both times. install.sh and the README now document both schemes. sw cache v40.

Built an overall funnel tracker for Applications (total applied + since-date, waiting on, heard back, rejected, reached out to — the last pulled from Networking Contacted count per Neil's call), shown as its own readable bar above the pipeline so nothing has to be searched for. Received Response day-batches now show a live outcome breakdown (e.g. Follow Up 1, Will Be In Touch 2, Rejected 1) right on the collapsed header. Also built scripts/build_tracker_xlsx.py: a color-coded, one-line-per-field Excel export of every application (company, position, status, outcome, dates, salary, contact, links) to ~/Downloads/Neil_Job_Tracker.xlsx, wired in as /jobs step 6 so it regenerates every run. Verified all of it against real data (46 real applications rendered correctly in the xlsx; tracker math hand-checked against seeded data in-browser). sw cache v39.

Fixed a real Gmail-check bug Neil caught live: a Willow Wealth rejection was misclassified as 'Will Be In Touch' because only the truncated search snippet was read instead of the full email body. Corrected that card, re-verified all 17 other Received Response cards by reading full bodies (all were actually correct), and hardened /jobs step 5 with a hard no-exceptions rule (full body required, citing this incident) plus a standing memory. Also polished the UI per Neil's follow-up ask: Received Response cards now sort Follow Up (green) before Will Be In Touch (amber) before Rejected (red) within each day, and every status section plus every day-batch across Applications and Networking now defaults collapsed instead of one section auto-opening. sw cache v38.

Added a new 'Received Response' status to the Applications pipeline (sits right after Applied), populated by a new step 5 in ~/.claude/commands/jobs.md: every /jobs run now searches Gmail (search_threads + get_thread) for replies on any 'Applied' or already-flagged 'Received Response' card, classifies the outcome (Rejected/Follow Up/Will Be In Touch, first-match-wins keyword rules), and writes gmailOutcome/gmailSubject/gmailSummary/gmailThreadId/gmailCheckedAt onto the card. Frontend: a colored badge (red/green/amber) shows the outcome before you even open anything, plus a 'Gmail update' dropdown with the subject/summary/checked-date and an Open in Gmail link. Batched per day using the same original posting date as To Apply. Re-checks already-flagged cards each run so an outcome can be superseded later. Verified the full UI with seeded Rejected/Follow Up/Will Be In Touch cards in-browser; the actual Gmail-search step itself will get its first real test on the next /jobs run. sw cache v37.

Root-caused the recurring “no new postings” gap: the Claude cloud routine's own hosted sandbox intermittently 403s every job-board domain at the network policy level, not a search-logic bug. Disabled the routine (trig_015uLUA1KJDwh46qALfPMQHq) rather than patch it again, and built ~/.claude/commands/jobs.md, a global /jobs command that runs the full sourcing-to-hub pipeline locally on demand (no sandbox network restrictions there).

Networking outreach now batches per day too, but by the linked application's posting date (matched via job link or company name against career-applications), not the contact's own created date — so outreach follows the same daily rhythm as the pipeline it's chasing. Falls back to an 'Undated' group for manually added contacts with no matching application. Same collapsible chevron UI, own localStorage key (career-networking-closed-batches). Verified in-browser with link-matched, company-matched, and unmatched contacts. sw cache v36.

Extended per-day grouping beyond To Apply: Applied now batches by the actual dateApplied, and Did Not Apply batches by the day it was declined (noApplyAt) — so answering Yes/No on a posting gives an accurate day-by-day record instead of one flat list. Same collapsible chevron UI, batch keys namespaced per status. Verified both Yes and No flows in-browser. sw cache v35.

Added a per-day collapse toggle to the Career > Applications > To Apply batches (chevron button, state persisted in localStorage so it survives reload). Verified in-browser with seeded test data across two dates, then cleaned it up. sw cache v34.

Fixed iPhone PWA: modals now portal to <body> (backdrop-filter on the sticky header was trapping the fixed sync dialog inside it), iOS-safe body scroll lock with position restore, safe-area insets for notch/home bar, 16px inputs to stop focus auto-zoom, card actions always visible on touch. Verified on a phone-sized viewport; sw cache v29.

Big cleanup session. Removed the standalone Ideas tab (ideas now live in Projects), changed the logo from NAH to NP, and rewrote all the project text in plain, everyday language. Added a stage filter at the top, a 'move to stage' option in each card's menu, bulleted notes, and a new 'open details' view with a journal. Also trimmed the stages down to just Idea / Building / Live, and added four new ideas (Mithoo game, runner game for my girlfriend, AI newsletter, algo trading).
Cross-device sync working — each device pulls and pushes its data through GitHub, so the hub stays in sync between my phone and Mac.
Built and launched the hub — a personal dashboard to keep an eye on all my AI projects in one place. Live on GitHub Pages.

data.name: Neil Portfolio
data.description: My personal portfolio website — about me, experience, skills, education, plus a little Q&A chat bot. It's live online.
data.lastDid: Checked that the site is live and working, and tidied up its info here.
data.nextStep: Pick a few finishing touches: a smarter chat bot, a downloadable resume, or fixing a location link that doesn't go anywhere.

log.text: Confirmed the site is live and working on GitHub Pages, and tidied up its info here in the hub. Ideas for finishing touches: a smarter chat bot, a downloadable resume, or fixing a location link that goes nowhere.
Built my personal portfolio site — about me, experience, skills, education, and a little Q&A chat bot. Launched it on GitHub Pages.

data.name: Job Search
data.description: Finance job search: middle-office analyst roles at reputable NYC/Jersey City firms, $80-100K+. Daily cloud digest + tracker + referral outreach.
data.lastDid: Finished this morning's /jobs run (Gmail check + tracker regen), but the Gmail check had a bug: it missed 2 real rejections (Citi, Brown Advisory) because they arrived as brand-new email threads rather than replies, and the re-check only looked at each card's already-known thread. Neil caught it manually. Fixed both cards in the hub, fixed the /jobs playbook so re-checks always re-search instead of re-fetching a stale thread ID.
data.nextStep: 24 cards in To Apply, 60 unsent networking messages - outreach is still the biggest lever. Also check Cerberus/Natixis/both Mondrian Alpha applications next run (only ever got LinkedIn's own confirmation, never a real employer reply), and check whether the newly-sourced Fiduciary Trust/Franklin Templeton Client Onboarding card is a dupe of an already-rejected 2026-07-03 application.

log.text: Neil caught 2 rejections (Citi, Brown Advisory) my Gmail check missed - they came as new threads, not replies. Fixed both cards and the /jobs step 5 process so re-checks re-search instead of re-fetching a stale thread ID.
Resumed and finished this morning's /jobs run: Gmail-checked all 28 sent applications (5 new acknowledgments), regenerated Neil_Job_Tracker.xlsx, pushed to hub.

Ran sourcing routine v3: added 10 new target postings (Alvarez & Marsal, Ramp, D.E. Shaw, Amex, JPMorganChase, Disney, Fiduciary Trust, Broadridge, Career Group Search recruiter role, Roku) to the To Apply column.

Hub UI v2 per Neil: one-section-at-a-time accordion, compact rows, Applied? Yes/No flow (No captures a reason and feeds the sourcing routine), Did Not Apply stage, batches grouped by date. Seeded Job Roles (12) and Companies (53, grouped by type/size) as aspirational target lists. Fixed fmtDate tz bug; mobile polish; sw v28.
Built hub job pipeline: To Apply column + resume/salary/deadline fields, seeded 6 fully-tailored applications, rewired daily routine to push postings into the hub instead of Gmail drafts.

data.name: Rendezvous — fair meetup finder
data.description: Finds a meetup spot for a friend group that is fair on travel time (not just geographic midpoint), with a fairness % score and review-filtered venue suggestions. Built entirely on free APIs (Nominatim, OpenRouteService, Overpass, Yelp) — no Google Maps, no card required.
data.nextStep: Neil: open https://rendezvous-smoky.vercel.app on your iPhone, add your real friends, and try Find a Spot for real. From there decide whether it needs magic-link auth (currently single-user, no login) before sharing with the friend group. Also still not pushed to a GitHub repo (currently local-only) — worth doing at some point.
data.lastDid: Built Rendezvous end-to-end over the last two days: v1 with Nominatim geocoding, Overpass venue search, ORS travel times, and Yelp ratings on Neon Postgres; then a full warm/friendly visual redesign (fixed a broken font fallback, lucide icons) restructuring the flow into homepage -> friend selection -> category planner with address autocomplete and sortable results; then map polish (clustered pins, auto-fit, a 'Let's go' native-share button, Closest sort, Yelp price level) plus a real map-crash fix; then homepage copy cleanup (dropped the how-it-works section, expanded and sharpened the rotating headlines). Deployed to Vercel (project 'rendezvous') but not yet pushed to a GitHub repo.
data.notes: Single-user v1 (no auth yet) — friends are manually added by the owner, no account needed for them, matches the "works even without a phone" requirement. Deliberately skipped Postgres/Neon and magic-link auth for now in favor of local SQLite to get a fully working app with zero external account setup; auth + real hosting is a planned next phase once this is validated with the friend group. API keys (ORS + Yelp) are stored as Vercel project env vars and in the local .env.local (gitignored, never in this public repo) — if ever lost, regenerate free at https://openrouteservice.org/dev/#/signup and https://www.yelp.com/developers/v3/manage_app. Stack: Next.js on Vercel, Neon Postgres, Nominatim (geocoding/autocomplete), Overpass (venue search), OpenRouteService (travel times), Yelp (ratings/price). Vercel project name is 'rendezvous'. Not yet pushed to GitHub -- only exists as a local git repo right now.

log.text: Expanded rotating homepage headlines (no immediate repeats) and sharpened copy.
Dropped the how-it-works section from the homepage and vertically centered the hero for a faster tap-to-enter landing.

Clustered overlapping map pins, auto-fit the map to friends+venues, added Closest sort and Yelp price level to result cards, added a 'Let's go' native-share button, and fixed a real map crash from a race between the auto-fit effect and the clustering plugin.

Warm/friendly visual redesign (fixed a broken font variable that was silently falling back to serif, lucide icons replacing emoji). Restructured the flow into homepage -> friend selection -> category planner, added address autocomplete, made results sortable by fairness or rating.

Migrated from local SQLite to free Neon Postgres (Vercel Marketplace) for deployability, wired both API keys as real Vercel env vars, deployed to production, and verified the full add-friend + find-a-spot flow live on the deployed URL.
Built Rendezvous v1: fair meetup finder using Nominatim geocoding, Overpass venue search, ORS travel times, and Yelp ratings on Neon Postgres.

Scaffolded and built v1 end-to-end on the free API stack (no Google Maps). Friends CRUD, meetup planner, fairness-scored results with map, all working except the two free API keys (ORS + Yelp) still need to be added.
